message.content:
The bell above the door jingled, cutting through the symphony of ticking that filled the shop. It wasn’t a single sound, but a layered rhythm—hundreds of clocks, each with its own heartbeat, creating a chaotic yet harmonious hum.

Leo, a boy of about eight with scraped knees and eyes wide as saucers, stepped inside. The air smelled of oil, old wood, and dust.

Behind a cluttered workbench sat Mr. Abernathy. He was so old he seemed to be made of the same leather and brass as the clocks around him. He wore a loupe over one eye, peering into the guts of a grandfather clock’s heart.

"Closed," Mr. Abernathy said, without looking up. His voice was like grinding gears.

"But the sign says 'Open,'" Leo insisted, pointing to the small chalkboard by the door.

Mr. Abernathy sighed, a sound like escaping steam. He set down his tweezers. "The sign is wrong. Time, however, is never wrong. Come in, then. But don’t touch anything. These aren’t toys; they are prisoners."

Leo blinked. "Prisoners?"

"Of seconds," the old man muttered, gesturing to a chair. "Sit. You have a question. I can hear it in your footsteps. They were too fast for the room."

Leo sat on the edge of the chair, swinging his legs. "I was watching the second hand on the big clock in the town square. It moves so slow. Tick... tick... tick. But when I’m playing tag, time flies. And when I’m waiting for my mom to pick me up, it’s stuck. Why does time change speed?"

Mr. Abernathy smiled, a cracking of dry parchment. "A classic mystery. You
